section.title: The Righteous and the Wicked
section.sectionRationale: Part of the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, this chapter repeatedly contrasts the boldness of the righteous with the flight of the wicked, the wisdom of keeping the law with the folly of forsaking it, and the futility of self-trust with the safety of walking in wisdom, pressing hard on confession, generosity, and the true source of national stability.
section.thesis: The righteous are as bold as a lion because they keep the law and trust the LORD, while the wicked flee even when no one pursues, since concealed sin never prospers but confessed and forsaken sin finds mercy, and a land is only as stable as its leaders' understanding and justice.
section.frameNote: Four clusters: the bold righteous and the fleeing wicked; keeping the law versus oppressing the poor; confession, generosity, and the danger of hasty wealth; self-trust versus walking in wisdom, and the wicked in power.

units.label: The bold righteous, the fleeing wicked

verses.text: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
A land in rebellion has many rulers, but a man of understanding and knowledge maintains order.
A destitute leader who oppresses the poor is like a driving rain that leaves no food.
Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law resist them.
Evil men do not understand justice, but those who seek the LORD comprehend fully.

units.label: Keeping the law, oppressing the poor
units.note: the discerning son, the deaf ear

verses.text: Better a poor man who walks with integrity than a rich man whose ways are perverse.
A discerning son keeps the law, but a companion of gluttons disgraces his father.
He who increases his wealth by interest and usury lays it up for one who is kind to the poor.
Whoever turns his ear away from hearing the law, even his prayer is detestable.
He who leads the upright along the path of evil will fall into his own pit, but the blameless will inherit what is good.

units.label: Confession, generosity, and hasty wealth
units.note: he who confesses and renounces will find mercy

verses.text: A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man with discernment sees through him.
When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, men hide themselves.
He who conceals his sins will not prosper, but whoever {{yadah|confesses}} and renounces them will find mercy.
Blessed is the man who is always reverent, but he who hardens his heart falls into trouble.
Like a roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a helpless people.
A leader who lacks judgment is also a great oppressor, but he who hates dishonest profit will prolong his days.
A man burdened by bloodguilt will flee into the Pit; let no one support him.
He who walks with integrity will be kept safe, but whoever is perverse in his ways will suddenly fall.
The one who works his land will have plenty of food, but whoever chases fantasies will have his fill of poverty.
A faithful man will abound with blessings, but one eager to be rich will not go unpunished.

units.label: Self-trust and the wicked in power
units.note: he who walks in wisdom will be safe

verses.text: To show partiality is not good, yet a man will do wrong for a piece of bread.
A stingy man hastens after wealth and does not know that poverty awaits him.
He who rebukes a man will later find more favor than one who flatters with his tongue.
He who robs his father or mother, saying, "It is not wrong," is a companion to the man who destroys.
A greedy man stirs up strife, but he who trusts in the LORD will prosper.
He who trusts in himself is a fool, but one who walks in {{chokmah|wisdom}} will be safe.
Whoever gives to the poor will not be in need, but he who hides his eyes will receive many curses.
When the wicked come to power, people hide themselves; but when they perish, the righteous flourish.

yadah.gloss: To confess, acknowledge, give thanks. Verse 13 pairs this confessing with forsaking sin as the path to mercy, contrasted with concealing sin, which never prospers, the same root used for confession of sin throughout Israel's worship and later echoed in 1 John 1:9.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living rightly before God. Verse 26 contrasts trusting oneself, called folly, with walking in this wisdom, called safety, showing that true wisdom is inseparable from humility and dependence rather than self-reliant confidence.

basic.context: This chapter draws sharp lines between the righteous and the wicked in nearly every verse: the bold lion versus the fleeing coward, the discerning son versus the disgraceful companion of gluttons, the one who confesses sin and finds mercy versus the one who conceals it and never prospers. It closes by insisting that trusting in oneself is folly, while walking in wisdom, and trusting the LORD, is safety.
basic.hermeneutics: These proverbs describe patterns of moral cause and effect under God's ordered world, not mechanical guarantees for every individual case. The chapter's repeated concern for national leadership, verses 2, 15, 16, and 28, shows these are not merely private virtues but matters with real consequences for whole communities under good or bad rulers.
basic.originalLanguages: Yadah, to confess or acknowledge, verse 13, names the honest, verbal admission of sin that, paired with actually forsaking it, opens the door to mercy, unlike concealment, which guarantees failure to prosper. Chokmah, wisdom, verse 26, is set directly against self-trust, teaching that true wisdom always involves looking outward and upward, never mere confidence in one's own judgment.
basic.typology: Verse 13's promise that the one who confesses and forsakes sin finds mercy anticipates the fuller gospel promise that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us, 1 John 1:9. The bold righteous, as fearless as a lion, verse 1, foreshadows the confidence believers have in Christ, who has removed condemnation and given boldness before God, Hebrews 4:16.
basic.christ: Verse 13's mercy for the one who confesses and forsakes sin, rather than conceals it, anticipates the gospel's own promise, that if we confess our sins, God who is faithful and just will forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness, 1 John 1:9, a mercy secured fully at the cross. And the bold, lion-like confidence of the righteous, verse 1, points to the confidence believers now have in Christ, who removes condemnation and lets them approach the throne of grace with boldness, Hebrews 4:16, Romans 8:1.
basic.god: God's justice orders both individual and national life; leaders who oppress the poor bring devastation like a driving rain that leaves no food, verse 3, while those who seek the LORD comprehend justice fully, verse 5. He offers real mercy to anyone who confesses and forsakes sin, verse 13, showing that His justice is never separated from His willingness to forgive the truly repentant.
basic.crossReferences: 1 John 1:9 fulfills the mercy promised to the one who confesses sin in verse 13. Hebrews 4:16 echoes the bold confidence of the righteous in verse 1. Romans 8:1 echoes the safety of walking rightly rather than trusting oneself, verse 26. James 5:16 develops the practice of confession commended in verse 13.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 28 contrasts bold, confessing righteousness with fleeing, concealing wickedness, and insists that trusting the LORD, not oneself, is the path of safety. This whole pattern comes to its fullest expression in Christ, through whom confessed sin finds certain mercy and through whom His people gain the same lion-like boldness before God that this chapter praises.

context.literary: Chapter 28 belongs to the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, and stands out for its unusually consistent antithetical structure, nearly every verse contrasting the righteous or wise with the wicked or foolish, often within a governmental or societal frame rather than purely private morality.
context.historical: Written in a context where kings and rulers, oppressive or just, directly shaped a nation's welfare, verses like 2, 15, 16, and 28 speak to the real political stakes of righteous versus wicked leadership in the ancient Near East, a concern Hezekiah's own scribes, compiling this collection, would have felt acutely given the turbulent reigns before and after him.
context.canonical: Verse 13's teaching on confession and mercy anchors a theme that runs throughout Scripture, from David's confession in Psalm 32 and 51 to the New Testament's full development in 1 John 1:9 and James 5:16.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To draw sharp, repeated contrasts between righteous and wicked conduct, in both personal life and national leadership, showing that confession and trust in the LORD lead to mercy and safety while concealment and self-reliance lead to ruin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses like 13 and 27 prescribe conduct directly, confess and forsake sin, give to the poor, while verses like 2 and 28 mainly describe how societal stability rises and falls with the quality of leadership, offering wisdom for evaluating and praying for good government rather than direct personal commands.

debates.question: Does verse 1, 'the righteous are as bold as a lion,' promise that genuine faith always produces fearless confidence, so that fear itself signals a lack of righteousness?

views.label: Yes, true righteousness should always manifest as fearless boldness
views.case: The verse's vivid contrast, the wicked flee even without pursuit while the righteous are bold as a lion, could be read as a diagnostic test, implying that persistent fear reveals unrighteousness.

views.label: It describes a general disposition of a clear conscience before God, not an absolute absence of all fear in every believer
views.case: Scripture shows genuinely righteous figures, such as Elijah, 1 Kings 19:3, and even Jesus in Gethsemane, Matthew 26:37-38, experiencing real fear or distress without forfeiting righteousness; the proverb's point is that a clear conscience under God's law removes the guilty dread that drives the wicked to flee without cause, not that righteous people never feel fear in the face of genuine danger.

debates.bearing: Reading the verse as describing the freedom of a clear conscience, rather than an absolute promise of fearlessness, protects believers who struggle with fear or anxiety from false guilt while still honoring the proverb's real point, that guilt and concealed sin produce a fearfulness integrity does not.

study.originalLanguages: Yadah, confess or acknowledge, verse 13, names honest, verbal admission of wrongdoing that, joined to actually forsaking the sin, secures mercy, in contrast to concealment, which guarantees failure. Chokmah, wisdom, verse 26, stands opposed to mere self-trust, insisting that real wisdom always looks beyond one's own judgment toward God and godly counsel.

typology.type: Mercy for the one who confesses and forsakes sin, 28:13
typology.fulfillment: The gospel promise of forgiveness for confessed sin, 1 John 1:9.

typology.type: The righteous bold as a lion, 28:1
typology.fulfillment: The believer's confident access to God through Christ, Hebrews 4:16, Romans 8:1.

study.christ: Verse 13's mercy promised to the one who confesses and forsakes sin, rather than concealing it, anticipates the fuller gospel assurance that if we confess our sins, God who is faithful and just will forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness, 1 John 1:9, mercy purchased and secured at the cross of Christ. The lion-like boldness of the righteous, verse 1, likewise finds its true ground in Christ, since there is now no condemnation for those in Him, Romans 8:1, and believers may approach the throne of grace with confidence, Hebrews 4:16, a boldness that flows not from human effort but from union with the crucified and risen Savior.
study.god: God's justice governs both personal integrity and national leadership; oppressive rulers bring ruin like a driving rain that leaves no food, verse 3, while those who seek Him understand justice fully, verse 5. He extends genuine mercy to anyone who confesses and forsakes sin, verse 13, revealing a character in which justice and forgiveness meet rather than compete.

crossReferences.note: If we confess our sins, He is faithful to forgive, fulfilling the mercy promised in Proverbs 28:13.
Approach the throne of grace with confidence, echoing the boldness of the righteous in Proverbs 28:1.
No condemnation for those in Christ Jesus, grounding the safety of Proverbs 28:26 in the gospel.
Confess your sins to one another, developing the confession commended in Proverbs 28:13.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 28 sets bold, confessing righteousness against fleeing, concealing wickedness, and insists that trusting the LORD rather than oneself is the true path of safety. All of this finds its fulfillment in Christ, through whom confessed sin secures certain mercy and through whom His people receive the same fearless confidence before God this chapter describes.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 say about the wicked and the righteous?

options.text: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
options.note: Guilt produces groundless fear, while a clear conscience produces confidence.

options.text: Both the wicked and righteous are equally fearful.
options.note: The verse draws a sharp contrast, not an equivalence.

options.text: The righteous flee more often than the wicked.
options.note: This directly reverses the verse's meaning.

options.text: The wicked are bold, while the righteous are fearful.
options.note: This exactly reverses the verse's actual comparison.

quiz.q: According to verse 9, what happens to the prayer of one who turns his ear from hearing the law?

options.text: Even his prayer is detestable.
options.note: Refusing to hear God's instruction corrupts even acts of worship like prayer.

options.text: It is answered more quickly than others.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's warning.

options.text: It remains neutral, neither helped nor harmed.
options.note: The verse gives a strong negative judgment, not neutrality.

options.text: It is only slightly less effective than normal.
options.note: The verse's language, 'detestable,' is much stronger than merely 'slightly less effective.'

quiz.q: What does verse 13 promise to the one who confesses and renounces sin?

options.text: He will find mercy, unlike the one who conceals sin and will not prosper.
options.note: Honest confession paired with actually forsaking sin leads to mercy.

options.text: He will still be punished regardless of confession.
options.note: The verse specifically promises mercy for confession joined with forsaking sin.

options.text: Confession alone, without changing behavior, guarantees mercy.
options.note: The verse pairs confession with actually forsaking the sin, not confession alone.

options.text: Concealing sin usually leads to eventual prosperity.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the verse states about concealment.

options.text: He who trusts in himself is a fool, but one who walks in wisdom will be safe.
options.note: True wisdom is set against mere self-confidence.

options.text: Self-trust and wisdom are ultimately the same thing.
options.note: This is the opposite of the sharp contrast the verse draws.

options.text: Trusting oneself is wise as long as one is intelligent.
options.note: The verse calls self-trust foolish regardless of intelligence.

options.text: Walking in wisdom is safe only for the wealthy.
options.note: The verse makes no distinction based on wealth; its point is about self-trust versus wisdom generally.

quiz.q: What does verse 27 say about giving to the poor?

options.text: Whoever gives to the poor will not be in need, but he who hides his eyes will receive many curses.
options.note: Generosity toward the poor is tied to blessing, while willful blindness to need is tied to curse.

options.text: Giving to the poor always leads to personal poverty.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's promise.

options.text: Ignoring the poor's needs has no real consequence.
options.note: The verse explicitly names a consequence, curses, for hiding one's eyes.

options.text: Only wealthy people are able to give to the poor meaningfully.
options.note: The verse makes no such qualification about the giver's wealth level.

questions: Verse 1 contrasts the fleeing wicked with the bold righteous. Is there guilt you are carrying right now that is producing groundless fear or restlessness in your life?
Verse 13 promises mercy to the one who confesses and forsakes sin, but no prosperity to the one who conceals it. What sin have you been hiding rather than honestly bringing into the light?
Verse 23 says a rebuke earns more favor in time than flattery. Have you recently received correction poorly, and how might you respond differently next time?
Verse 26 calls self-trust foolish and walking in wisdom safe. Where are you currently relying on your own judgment instead of seeking godly counsel or God's own word?
Verse 27 promises blessing to the generous and curses to those who hide their eyes from need. Whose need have you been avoiding seeing lately?

translationNotes.note: 'A land in rebellion has many rulers' describes political instability producing a rapid succession of leaders, a warning tied to the historical experience of Israel and Judah's turbulent royal history.

'Confesses and renounces' translates two paired Hebrew verbs, acknowledging wrongdoing and actually turning from it, showing that mere verbal admission without change is not the full picture of biblical confession.
'Will flee into the Pit' likely refers to Sheol or the grave, and 'let no one support him' warns against enabling or protecting someone guilty of bloodguilt from just consequence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 28. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
